title: [CI:DOCS] Add a hello world image to quay.io
body:
[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

This commit describes how to run the quay.io/podman/hello
image.  It also contains the files necessary to build that
image localy, and a README.md explaining the image and how
to build it.

Signed-off-by: tomsweeneyredhat <tsweeney@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]".  That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name.  Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *TomSweeneyRedHat* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [TomSweeneyRedHat] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
White page checks failures on README.md files have been driving me insane lately.  Is there a good reason to keep that and can we toggle it off for that file name?  @edsantiago  do you know?

@TomSweeneyRedHat I'm having trouble parsing your question. My best guess is you mean "whitespace checks", as in, trailing whitespace? If so, no, I don't know any way to disable it, nor would I want to. Trailing whitespace is evil, and does not belong in any repo. There are tools for [emacs](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31036226/enabling-whitespace-mode-on-emacs) and [vi](https://vim.fandom.com/wiki/Highlight_unwanted_spaces) that can help detect and fix trailing whitespace.

If I've misunderstood your question, could I ask you to rephrase it?

My question was to turn off white space checks for README.md and/or just *.md files.  I don't see the inherent evil in an extra whitespace at the end of a text line, 10,000 maybe, but not one or two.  

I've not tried the vi macros in a while, I had problems with them being too ambitious or just not working properly in the past, perhaps I should investigate.

> I think we should keep this simple. Pulling for 10 more seconds is not a big issue.

I think it is easy to go overboard with these things, but also interesting to compare.

I made a small shell script, and added it to the base image:

```shell
#!/bin/sh
cat <<'EOF'
 _  _     _ _        __      __       _    _ _ 
| || |___| | |___    \ \    / /__ _ _| |__| | |
| __ / -_) | / _ \_   \ \/\/ / _ \ '_| / _` |_|
|_||_\___|_|_\___( )   \_/\_/\___/_| |_\__,_(_)
                 |/                            
EOF
```

```Dockerfile
FROM docker.io/fedora
COPY hello-world.sh /usr/local/bin/hello-world
CMD ["/usr/local/bin/hello-world"]
```

```
REPOSITORY             TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED             SIZE
localhost/hello-world  fedora      464de1fad4f0  About a minute ago  159 MB
localhost/hello-world  alpine      8214c038a5d9  About a minute ago  5.87 MB
localhost/hello-world  busybox     953ab735f129  About a minute ago  1.47 MB
localhost/hello-world  static      5f190908c84c  13 minutes ago      88.2 kB
```

If this container is the _only_ one running, that is a 100x difference.
You start saving on the _second_ container using the OS base image.

For the "static" (based on scratch), I converted it into a binary (now arch-dependent!)

```C
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    puts("\
 _  _     _ _        __      __       _    _ _ \n\
| || |___| | |___    \\ \\    / /__ _ _| |__| | |\n\
| __ / -_) | / _ \\_   \\ \\/\\/ / _ \\ '_| / _` |_|\n\
|_||_\\___|_|_\\___( )   \\_/\\_/\\___/_| |_\\__,_(_)\n\
                 |/                            ");
}
```

```Dockerfile
FROM docker.io/alpine as builder
RUN apk add gcc libc-dev
ADD hello-world.c .
RUN gcc -O2 -static -o hello-world hello-world.c

FROM scratch
COPY --from=builder hello-world /usr/local/bin/hello-world
CMD ["/usr/local/bin/hello-world"]

```

----

And if you _really_ want to go crazy, you can write the thing in assembler instead.

```
REPOSITORY                     TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED       SIZE
docker.io/library/hello-world  latest      feb5d9fea6a5  4 months ago  19.9 kB
```

@afbjorklund I'd not thought about the C route.  I tried golang to start, but that was well over 1.2GB.  I tried Python next, but that wasn't  much better at about 800MB.  The C and the assembler might be entertaining.  The scratch trick is neat too, might have to play with that.  Thanks!

@TomSweeneyRedHat : to distro or not to distro, that is the question...

https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/why-distroless-containers-arent-security-solution-you-think-they-are

One thing that is potentially useful for small containers is a libc image.

https://boot2podman.github.io/2019/01/02/tiny-init-minimal.html

----

Probably want to use glibc rather than uclibc, in that case.

```
REPOSITORY   TAG       IMAGE ID       CREATED       SIZE
busybox      glibc     99f6700cca09   4 weeks ago   4.79MB
busybox      musl      b58b4dc0c628   4 weeks ago   1.43MB
busybox      uclibc    beae173ccac6   4 weeks ago   1.24MB
busybox      latest    beae173ccac6   4 weeks ago   1.24MB
```

If using the dynamic libc, rather than linking static (like musl above)
I think converting it to C and then building a static executable is the best solution and fixes size + maintenance of the image.

I am going to merge, and then others can improve.

/lgtm
